---
title: "Introduction to the sf Package"
subtitle: "DMgtAsia · Session-III"
format:
  live-html:
    toc: true
  live-revealjs:
    output-file: slides-session3-intro-sf.html
    theme: [default, dmgt.scss]
    logo: dmgt_asia_logo.png
    footer: "DMgtAsia · Spatial Epidemiology"
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    slide-number: c/t
    show-slide-number: all
    progress: true
    transition: fade
    scrollable: true
    incremental: false
webr:
  packages:
    - sf
    - ggplot2
engine: knitr
other-links:
  - text: "View as slides"
    href: slides-session3-intro-sf.html
    icon: easel2
---

## The Simple Features standard

-   `sf` implements **Simple Features**, the ISO standard for representing geometry.
-   An `sf` object is a data frame with a special **geometry** column.
-   So spatial data is just tidy data with a place attached: one row per feature, columns of
    attributes, plus geometry.

::: aside
Live R cells on this page run in your browser via **webR**. The first cell takes a few seconds
while `sf` loads; after that they are quick.
:::

---

## Geometry types

-   **POINT** a single location (a clinic, a case).
-   **LINESTRING** a path (a road, a river).
-   **POLYGON** an area (a district, a PHC catchment).
-   Each has a **MULTI** form (`MULTIPOLYGON` for a state made of many parts).

---

## Build an sf object (live)

Press **Run**. We make points for four cities, then attach geometry.

```{webr}
library(sf)

cities <- data.frame(
  name = c("Trivandrum", "Bibinagar", "Mumbai", "Kolkata"),
  lon  = c(76.95, 78.83, 72.88, 88.36),
  lat  = c(8.52, 17.45, 19.08, 22.57)
)

cities_sf <- st_as_sf(cities, coords = c("lon", "lat"), crs = 4326)
cities_sf
```

Note the `geometry` column and the printed CRS header.

---

## Quiz: making an sf object

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof q1 = Inputs.radio(
  ["st_read()", "st_as_sf()", "st_transform()", "st_crs()"],
  { label: "Which function converts a data frame with lon/lat columns into an sf object?" }
);
html`${q1 == null ? "" : q1 === "st_as_sf()" ? "<strong>Correct.</strong> st_as_sf() turns a data frame with coordinate columns into an sf object." : "<em>Not quite.</em> Which function turns lon/lat columns into geometry?"}`
```

---

## Coordinate reference systems

-   A CRS tells R what the coordinates mean.
-   **Geodetic** (EPSG:4326, WGS 84): longitude/latitude in **degrees**. Good for storage and
    web maps.
-   **Projected** (a UTM zone such as EPSG:32643): **metres** on a flat plane. Needed for
    distances and areas.

---

## Inspect and transform (live)

```{webr}
st_crs(cities_sf)$epsg          # current CRS code

cities_utm <- st_transform(cities_sf, 32643)   # to UTM 43N (metres)
st_coordinates(cities_utm)
```

The numbers change from degrees to metres after `st_transform()`.

---

## Quiz: what is EPSG:4326?

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof q2 = Inputs.radio(
  ["Geodetic, in degrees", "Projected, in metres", "Always a UTM zone", "An equal-area projection"],
  { label: "EPSG:4326 (WGS 84) is..." }
);
html`${q2 == null ? "" : q2 === "Geodetic, in degrees" ? "<strong>Correct.</strong> EPSG:4326 (WGS 84) is geodetic: longitude/latitude in degrees." : "<em>Try again.</em> 4326 is the familiar lon/lat system."}`
```

---

## Plot an sf object (live)

```{webr}
library(ggplot2)

ggplot(cities_sf) +
  geom_sf(size = 3) +
  theme_minimal()
```

`geom_sf()` reads the geometry column directly. The same layer draws points, lines, or
polygons.

---

## Reading spatial files

In RStudio you read a shapefile or GeoJSON from disk:

```r
library(sf)
library(here)
india <- st_read(here("spatial_files", "India_State_Boundary.shp"))
st_crs(india)
```

You do this in **Exercise 1**. We keep file reading in RStudio because the data files are
large for a browser session.

---

## What runs live, and what does not

-   **Live in the browser (webR):** `sf` core, `st_as_sf`, `st_crs`, `st_transform`, `geom_sf`.
-   **Run in RStudio (static here):**
    -   Raster extraction (`exactextractr`, `raster`) in Exercise 3.
    -   Geocoding (`tidygeocoder`) in Exercise 4.
    -   Spatial weights and clustering (`spdep`, `rgeoda`, Moran's I, LISA) in **Session-IV**.

These packages are not yet built for WebAssembly, so they stay in the desktop exercises.

---

## Hands-on for this session

-   **Exercise 1** load and plot India shapefiles; check and transform CRS.
-   **Exercise 2** a medical-college choropleth; joins and `case_when` name harmonisation.
-   **Exercise 3** raster extraction (population per state).
-   **Exercise 4** geocoding medical colleges.

Each is an R script you run in RStudio.

## Recap

-   `sf` is tidy data with a geometry column.
-   Geometry types: point, line, polygon, and their multi forms.
-   `st_as_sf` builds geometry; `st_crs` reads it; `st_transform` reprojects.
-   `geom_sf` maps it.
-   Heavier tools (raster, geocoding, clustering) run in RStudio and in Session-IV.
